coredata.dc:title: Preparation of magnesium, nitrogen-codoped carbon quantum dots from lignin with bright green fluorescence and sensitive pH response
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xiaoxu
Hou, Shiyao
Chu, Tingting
Han, Jiazhi
Li, Runsen
Guo, Yanzhu
Gong, Yumei
Li, Haiming
Wan, Zhiwei
coredata.dc:description: Eco-friendly, renewable and green biomass materials are considered as the superior carbon precursors for producing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in large scale. A major challenge is the limited contents of fluorophores in biomass, which then results in low quantum yields (QYs) and inferior fluorescence emission ability of the obtained CQDs. Herein, lignin with amounts of unsaturated aromatic and carbonyl fluorophores was employed as the carbon precursors to prepare CQDs through a green, facile approach. The magnesium and nitrogen elements were successfully introduced into CQDs with the aim of enhancing their QYs and extending their emission wavelengths. The prepared magnesium, nitrogen-doped CQDs (Mg, N-CQDs) could emit green fluorescence with a quantum yield (QYs) up to 46.38 %. The mechanism of their fluorescence ability was discussed extensively. More interestingly, the Mg, N-CQDs displayed a fluorescence transition between green and yellow color at pH value of 5.4, indicating their potential application as a probe to detect pH values of complicated environments. The findings in this paper would provide a viable strategy to converse lignin biomass waste into high-quality fluorescence nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Hydrothermal treatment
Green fluorescence
pH response

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Mg, N-CQDs from CCEL
Morphology and chemical compositions of Mg, N-CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation ( 22078036 and 31500493 ), Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program ( XLYC1807232 ), Liaoning BaiQianWan Talents Program , the Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control ( 2019KF30 ) and the State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering (South China University of Technology , No. 202002 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PL emission spectra of N-CQDs (a) and Mg-CQDs (b) excited at various incident wavelengths; PL emission spectra (405 nm excitation) of Mg, N-CQDs with different dosages of Mg (OH)2 (c) and EDA (d) (The black and red lines in Fig. 1c are corresponding to the PL emission spectra (excitation at 405 nm) of N-CQDs and Mg, N-CQDs, respectively).
XRD patterns of N-CQDs, Mg-CQDs and Mg, N-CQDs (a); size distribution histogram (b), TEM (c) and HRTEM image (d) of Mg, N-CQDs.
FT-IR spectra and XPS survey spectra of Mg, N-CQDs; C1s (c), O1s (d), N1s (e) and Mg2p (f) high resolution XPS spectra of Mg, N-CQDs.

UV–vis absorbance spectra of N-CQDs, Mg-CQDs and Mg, N-CQDs samples (a), insets are the images of Mg, N-CQDs aqueous solution with a pH value of 6.1 under daylight (left) and UV light at 365 nm (right); excitation and emission spectra of the Mg, N-CQDs (b); emission spectra of Mg, N-CQDs excited by various incident lights progressively from 350 to 405 nm (c) and 400 to 420 nm (d).
Fluorescence mechanism of the Mg, N-CQDs.
Oxidation scanning of Mg, N-CQDs (a) and N-CQDs (b).
Photographs of Mg, N-CQDs solutions with various pH values under UV light.
Emission spectra (a), normalized emission spectra (b) and UV–vis absorbance spectra (c) of Mg, N-CQDs aqueous solutions with various pH values.

head.ce:title: Preparation of magnesium, nitrogen-codoped carbon quantum dots from lignin with bright green fluorescence and sensitive pH response

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Eco-friendly, renewable and green biomass materials are considered as the superior carbon precursors for producing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in large scale. A major challenge is the limited contents of fluorophores in biomass, which then results in low quantum yields (QYs) and inferior fluorescence emission ability of the obtained CQDs. Herein, lignin with amounts of unsaturated aromatic and carbonyl fluorophores was employed as the carbon precursors to prepare CQDs through a green, facile approach. The magnesium and nitrogen elements were successfully introduced into CQDs with the aim of enhancing their QYs and extending their emission wavelengths. The prepared magnesium, nitrogen-doped CQDs (Mg, N-CQDs) could emit green fluorescence with a quantum yield (QYs) up to 46.38 %. The mechanism of their fluorescence ability was discussed extensively. More interestingly, the Mg, N-CQDs displayed a fluorescence transition between green and yellow color at pH value of 5.4, indicating their potential application as a probe to detect pH values of complicated environments. The findings in this paper would provide a viable strategy to converse lignin biomass waste into high-quality fluorescence nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), with the internal hydrophobic core enfolded by the plentiful functional groups, have attracted tremendous attentions for their unique properties and widely application prospects (Baker and Baker, 2010; Park et al., 2014). In comparison with organic fluorophores and conventional quantum dots (QDs), CQDs are potentially to be used as nanosensors and nanoprobes owning to their high biocompatibility, excellent solubility and easily to be functionalization (Gao et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2014; Li et al., 2011). Therefore, extensively researches have been performed on preparation and application of CQDs in the past years and varieties of carbon precursors, mainly including small organic molecules and biomass materials, have been employed in the preparation of CQDs (Ding et al., 2016; Rao et al., 2018). In contrast to the small organic molecules, the biomass materials own some favorable properties, e.g., lower cost, eco-friendly and renewable (An et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020a,b; Dai et al., 2020a,b), which enable them to be the ideal source to prepare CQDs and intensive works have been focused on generation of CQDs from biomass materials. However, the reported CQDs from biomass materials usually possess low yield and poor optical properties, whose quantum yields (QYs) are commonly less than 20 % (Meng et al., 2019). Indeed, so low QYs are far inferior to those of traditional quantum dots and CQDs from small organic molecules. Furthermore, the emission spectra of CQDs from biomass are generally in the low wavelength and the CQDs could emit blue light, which eventually hinders the application of CQDs form biomass in the fields of sensor and imaging. Therefore, exploring an effective way to enhance QYs of CQDs and extend their emission spectra to long wavelength is urgent and very important.

In common, the photoluminescence (PL) properties of CQDs mainly generate from the radiative recombination of excitons in the fluorophore groups on the surface of CQDs, rather than the carbon core. This phenomenon is generally due to that the structure of carbon core is not pure graphite structure and the function of carbon core usually is hidden by the fluorophore-based chemical groups (Ding et al., 2016; Tao et al., 2017). The fluorophore groups, including heteroatom containing double bonds (CO, CN, NO, SO) and single bonds (NH, CN, CO), are the fluorescence centers in the CQDs and mainly generating from the chemical reaction during the preparation of CQDs or the functional groups of carbon precursors. Up to now, introduction of heteroatoms into CQDs has been employed to enhance the fluorophore groups of CQDs with the aim of regulating their fluorescence emission properties. Owing to the particular quantum confinement and edge effects, passivation of heteroatoms into CQDs through chemically bonds would provide active vacancies in CQDs and thus greatly alter their intrinsic characteristics, i.e., generate novel and extraordinary optical performance. Thus, more attention has been paid onto preparation of multicolor CQDs via introducing extraneous elements into carbon cores of CQDs (Huang et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2004). The typical employed elements include nitrogen, sulfur (Dang et al., 2018), magnesium (Bhati et al., 2018; Li et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2016) and fluorine, which are not limited to these. Magnesium hydroxide (Mg(OH)2) is usually chosen as a chelation agent to protect the carboxyl groups of carbon precursors during the procedure of dehydration and carbonization, which then leads to more fluorophore groups on the surface of CQDs (Bhati et al., 2018; Li et al., 2014). In addition, the present of magnesium in CQDs would cause the formation of new absorption bands, which would be a new fluorescence center in the PL emission.

In addition, the conjugations between fluorophore groups and unsaturated bonds of carbon precursors are confirmed to elevate the electron cloud density of CQDs and eventually lead to their efficient fluorescence emission ability. Therefore, the carbon precursors with unsaturated polycyclic aromatic structure are considered as the most reliable precursors to prepare CQDs with excellent fluorescence ability (Eskalen and Uruş, 2020; Zhang et al., 2019a, b). Lignin, one of the most abundant natural sources with the aromatic structure on the earth, is composed by phenyl skeletons and oxygen containing branches (Chen et al., 2020a,b; Dai et al., 2020a,b; Huang et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021). However, the value of lignin is not extensively exploitation, most of which is considered as a biomass waste in pulping industry and generally burned to generate energy. In the past decades, lignin and lignosulfonate have been reported as carbon precursors to prepare of CQDs in some literatures (Dang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019a, b). Unfortunately, the reported CQDs from lignin usually have amounts of defects, whose fluorescence are limited to blue light with low QYs. Furthermore, the process for preparing CQDs is usually tedious. Therefore, further effort is required to precisely design and control the surface fluorophore groups on the surface of CQDs, with the aim of resulting bright and multicolor CQDs from lignin.

In this paper, a green and facile approach to prepare magnesium, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (Mg, N-CQDs) with bright green fluorescence was demonstrated. The lignin, derived from the black liquor during the process of ethanol-water pulping of corncob, was employed as the carbon precursor. The condition for preparing Mg, N-CQDs was optimized with the aim of deriving the Mg, N-CQDs with the best fluorescence properties. The optical performances of Mg, N-CQDs solutions with various pH values were also evaluated. The present work not only provides green, low-cost and sustainable resources for generating high-quality fluorescent CQDs, but also gives added values to lignin-based biomass waste.

Corncob was harvested from local farms (Dalian, China). Magnesium hydroxide (Mg(OH)2, 99 %), quinine sulfate dehydrates (99 %), 9, 10-diphenylacetylene anthracene (99 %), citric acid (99 %) and sodium citrate (99 %) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The dialysis bag (MWCO 100−500 Da) was obtained from Spectrumlabs (America). Ethanol (99.9 %), ethanediamine (EDA, 99 %) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99 %) were obtained from Kermel Analytical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China).

Ethanol-water pulping lignin (CCEL) was supplied by the home-made black liquor in the process of ethanol-water pulping from corncob. In a typical experiment, 300 g corncob (7.15 wt. % moisture content) was weighed and charged into the digester. The ratio of corncob weight (dry wt. basis) to pulping liquor volume was constant at 1:6 and the concentration of ethanol in the cooking liquor was 60 % by volume. The pulping of corncob was performed at 180 °C for 2 h. The crude CCEL was obtained by precipitation of black liquor into distilled water, isolated through filtration using a 0.45 μm membrane and then dried in an oven.

After dissolution of crude CCEL into NaOH solution (0.001 M), the pH of the solution was then adjusted to 2.0 using HCl solution (2.0 M), which reduced solubility of the CCEL and facilitated its precipitation. After centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 30 min using a centrifuge (Sorvall ST 16, Thermo Fisher), the derived precipitates were then washed with distilled water for several times until the pH value of precipitates was close to 7.0. After drying at 105 °C in an oven for 12 h, the final pure CCEL was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Mg, N-CQDs from CCEL
ce:section.ce:para: Approximately, 0.5 g CCEL was dispersed into 5 mL deionized water and then the suspension was treated in a JY92-ⅡDN probe ultrasonicator (SCIENTZ, Ningbo, China) with a 75 W power for 10 min. Subsequently, 0−0.2 g magnesium hydroxide and 0−0.3 mL EDA were gradually added into the suspensions under continuous magnetic stirring with a stirring rate of 500 rpm. The mixtures were then transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 240 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the suspensions were cooled in a fridge at 0−5 °C for 2 h and then vacuum filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane. The yellow supernatants were transferred into a dialysis bag (MWCO 100−500 Da) and dialyzed against deionized water for three days. The purified carbon dots were finally dried in a vacuum dryer for two days and named as Mg, N-CQDs.

The characterization of Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of Mg, N-CQDs and CCEL were analyzed on a Fourier spectrometer (Frontier I, PerkinElmer, America) using the grinding KBr powder as background. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Mg, N-CQDs was collected on a 6100 X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu, Japan) using a Cu Kα as radiation source (40 kV, 30 mA) in the range of 10° to 60°. The X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectra of Mg, N-CQDs were collected on an AMICUS photoelectron spectroscopy (Shimadzu, Japan) with Mg Kα as the exciting source. The morphology of Mg, N-CQDs was observed on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The UV–vis absorbance spectra of Mg, N-CQDs were obtained on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV1006M031, Agilent Technologies Inc., America). The fluorescence spectra of Mg, N-CQDs samples were collected on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) in a quartz cell of 1 cm path length. The two-dimensional heteronuclear single quantum coherence nuclear magnetic resonance (2D HSQC NMR) spectrum of CCEL was recorded on an AVII 400 MHz spectrometer (Bruker, Switzerland) at 25 °C with DMSO-d 6 as the solvent. The molecular weights (Mw) and distribution for CCEL sample was analyzed using gel permeation chromatography (GPC, Malvern, GPCmax VE2001 Module Viscotek TDA305, The United Kingdom).

The Mg, N-CQDs were successfully prepared by one-step hydrothermal method using CCEL as carbon source, Mg(OH)2 as dopant and ethanediamine (EDA) as passivator. The CCEL was isolated from corncob using ethanol-water pulping method and its structure is characterized by the 2D HSQC NMR and GPC techniques. As shown in Fig. S7, signals from p-hydroxyphenyl (H), guaiacyl (G), syringyl (S), ferulate (FA) and p-coumarate (pCA) units (Chen et al., 2020a,b; Dai et al., 2020a,b; Liu et al., 2021) were easily observed in the 2D HSQC NMR spectrum of CCEL, confirming the present of G, S, H, FA and pCA units in the structure of CCEL. The linkages in the structure of the CCEL were mainly in the types of resinol (β-β), β-aryl ether (β-O-4) and phenylcoumaran (β-5) linkages. As shown in Fig. S8, the weight and number average molecular weight of CCEL was 8738 g/mol and 1468 g/mol, respectively. Effects of the dosages of Mg(OH)2 and EDA on the QYs and emission spectra of Mg, N-CQDs were investigated in detail and the results are shown in Fig. 1 . Simultaneously, to gain a better understanding of the roles of Mg(OH)2 and EDA in the formation of Mg, N-CQDs, two parallel samples were also prepared. The former sample, called as N-CQDs, was prepared by hydrothermal treatment of CCEL using EDA as surface passivation reagent without addition of Mg(OH)2, while the latter sample, named as Mg-CQDs, was prepared by hydrothermal treatment of CCEL with only addition of Mg(OH)2. The preparation temperature and heating time of two parallel samples were 240 °C and 10 h, which were as same as the conditions for preparing the Mg, N-CQDs.

As shown in Fig. 1a, the N-CQDs exhibited a maximum excitation at 320 nm and emission at 425 nm in its fluorescence spectra. According to Eq. S1, the QYs of N-CQDs was calculated to be 16.9 %. A weak emission peak at 430 nm, which was within the range of emission wavelength from blue light, was also observed in the emission spectra of N-CQDs as it was excited at 405 nm. As shown in Fig. 1c, with addition of Mg(OH)2, the emission peak of Mg, N-CQDs excitation at 405 nm was red-shifted from 430 nm to 500 nm, indicating that the peak at 500 nm was probably originated from the new fluorescence center in Mg, N-CQDs. It was also observed that the emission peak of Mg, N-CQDs was gradually red-shifting with increase of the dosage of Mg(OH)2, which was simultaneously accompanied by remarkable increase in PL intensity. The results indicated that the dosage of Mg(OH)2 was an vital factor for the maximum emission peak of Mg, N-CQDs. As depicted in Fig. 1b, a weak and broad emission peak at 485 nm was observed in the emission spectra of Mg-CQDs as it was excited at 405 nm. As shown in Fig. 1d, the PL intensity of Mg, N-CQDs was remarkably increased with increasing the dosage of EDA from 0 mL/g to 0.4 mL/g. However, the PL intensity was decreased with further enhancing the dosage of EDA from 0.4 mg/mL to 0.6 mL/g. These results indicated that the dosage of EDA was an important factor to influence the QYs of Mg, N-CQDs. The optimized conditions were found to be 240 °C, 10 h heating time, 0.4 mL/g EDA to CCEL and 0.2 g/g Mg(OH)2 to CCEL. Under these conditions, the QYs value of generated Mg, N-CQDs was as high as 46.38 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical compositions of Mg, N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Mg, N-CQDs with a QY of 46.38 % was selected as the sample for further analysis by TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS techniques. As shown in Fig. 2 a, a broad diffraction peak at 22°∼30°, ascribing to the amorphous carbon, was observed in the XRD patterns of N-CQDs, Mg-CQDs and Mg, N-CQDs samples, indicating that there were disordered carbons in the structures of these three CQDs (Ding et al., 2016; Tao et al., 2017). The TEM image in Fig. 2c and d obviously confirmed the spherical morphology of the Mg, N-CQDs nanoparticles, which exhibited an average diameter of 7.83 nm ± 0.851 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2d) verified the present of crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm in the Mg, N-CQDs, which was attributed to the (1100) lattice fringes of graphene as previously reported CQDs (Gao et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2014). In addition, two fractions of the crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm and 0.25 nm could be observed in the HRTEM images (Fig. 2d), which are attributed to the (1120) lattice fringe of sp2 clusters and the (100) lattice fringes of graphite, respectively (Tao et al., 2017).

In the FT-IR spectrum of Mg, N-CQDs (Fig. 3 a), the peak at 1546 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of the aromatic CC group, while the peaks at 2867 cm−1 and 760 cm−1 are attributed to the in-plane and out-plane deformation vibrations of aromatic C–H group (Zhang et al., 2019a, b). These characteristic peaks demonstrated the presence of the aromatic rings in the structure of the Mg, N-CQDs. Comparing with that of CCEL, two new peaks at 1667 and 1343 cm−1 were observed in the spectrum of Mg, N-CQDs, which are ascribed to the stretching vibration of NH and CN groups, respectively (Dang et al., 2018; Peng et al., 2010). These results indicated that the nitrogen-based functional groups were present in the structure of the Mg, N-CQDs. The peaks at 3741 cm−1 and 574 cm−1 are attributed to the A2u lattice vibration and metal-ligand bending vibration of magnesium, respectively (Choudhary et al., 2015; Pareek et al., 2014), confirming the success passivation of magnesium into the Mg, N-CQDs. The peak at 3400 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of −OH group, was weakened in the spectrum of Mg, N-CQDs, indicating that amounts of phenolic hydroxyl groups was reacted with Mg(OH)2 and EDA during the hydrothermal treatment of CCEL.

The XPS spectrum of Mg, N-CQDs (Fig. 3b) displayed four peaks at 400.1 eV, 1304 eV, 89 eV and 56 eV, which are corresponding to the N1s, Mg1s, Mg2s and Mg2p, respectively (Bhati et al., 2018; Choudhary et al., 2015; Pareek et al., 2014). All of these four peaks were not observed in the XPS spectrum of CCEL (Fig. S1). The CCEL owned two elements, including 80.3 % of carbon and 19.7 % of oxygen, while the Mg, N-CQDs exhibited four elements, including 64.81 % of carbon, 19.97 % of oxygen, 12.99 % nitrogen and 2.23 % of magnesium. All of the above results verified that the nitrogen and magnesium elements were successfully doped into the Mg, N-CQDs.

The C1s high-resolution spectrum of Mg, N-CQDs (Fig. 3c) exhibited four peaks at 283.5, 284.5, 285.7 and 287.6 eV, ascribing to the C-Mg, CC/CC, CO/CN and CO bonds, respectively. Deconvolutions of the O1s peak in the Mg, N-CQDs and CCEL samples are shown in Figs. 3d and S2, respectively. As shown in Fig. S2, the O1s high-resolution spectrum of CCEL exhibited two peaks at 531.0 eV and 532.2 eV, which are attributed to the CO and CO bonds, respectively (Yuan et al., 2015). In comparison with that of CCEL, the high-resolution O1s spectrum of Mg, N-CQDs exhibited one new peak at 530.2 eV, attributing to the O-Mg bond (Li et al., 2014), which further confirmed the success doping of magnesium element into the Mg, N-CQDs. It was observed that the atomic percentage of CO carbon in Mg, N-CQDs was 48.4 % and higher than 29.83 % in CCEL, indicating that the oxidation of lignin probably occurred during the process of hydrothermal treatment.

The N1s high-resolution spectrum of Mg, N-CQDs (Fig. 3e) exhibited three peaks at 399.2, 400.7 and 401.5 eV, which are assigned to the C-N-C, N-(C)3 and NH bonds, respectively (Dang et al., 2018). It was observed that the atomic percentage of C-N-C nitrogen in Mg, N-CQDs was 77.5 %, indicating that most of the nitrogen atom on the surface of Mg, N-CQDs might be present in the form of C-N-C group. Meanwhile, the highly resolved Mg2p spectrum of Mg, N-CQDs (Fig. 3f) was deconvoluted into two peaks at 48.4 and 49.9 eV, which are assigned to Mg-C and Mg-O species, respectively. These results revealed that the Mg element was connected with carbon and oxygen atoms through metal binding sites (Choudhary et al., 2015; Pareek et al., 2014).

As shown in Fig. 4 a, the aqueous solution of Mg, N-CQDs with a pH value of 6.1 could emit strong green fluorescence under UV light radiation (excitation at 365 nm), which was transparent and yellowish under day light. The N-CQDs and Mg-CQDs aqueous solutions also were yellowish, clear and transparent under daylight (Fig. S3), meanwhile both of them could emit blue fluorescence under UV lamp (excitation at 365 nm). As shown in Fig. 4a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Mg, N-CQDs displayed a shoulder peak at 276 nm, belonged to the π→π* transition of the aromatic domains in the core of the Mg, N-CQDs (Gan et al., 2016). The same peak was also observed in the UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-CQDs and Mg-CQDs aqueous solutions. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CQDs solution exhibited a weak shoulder peak at 360 nm, attributing to the n-π* transition of CO group (Miao et al., 2017). Furthermore, the spectrum of Mg-CQDs did not display any absorption peak below 300 nm. Importantly, the spectrum of Mg, N-CQDs displayed a strong and wide peak at 410 nm, which is ascribed to unique charge transfer transition of the metal ligand in the Mg, N-CQDs.

The Mg, N-CQDs exhibited a maximum excitation at 405 nm and emission at 510 nm in its fluorescence spectra (Fig. 4b). As shown in Fig. 4c, the emission peak of the Mg, N-CQDs was quickly red-shifting from 420 nm to 490 nm and the fluorescence intensity of the Mg, N-CQDs gradually increased, when the excitation wavelength altered from 350 nm to 390 nm. Further elevating the excitation wavelength from 390 to 420 nm (Fig. 4d), the emission peak at 510 nm was scarcely changed, confirming that the luminescence center of Mg, N-CQDs was fixed at 510 nm.

Based on the above results, it was found that the Mg, N-CQDs and N-CQDs exhibited similar UV–vis spectra, but different PL spectra. Therefore, it could be concluded that there were very differences in the fluorescence spectra and fluorophores between these two samples. The fluorescence mechanism for these two samples were extensively explored with the aim of finding the causes for this phenomenon. As shown in Figs. S4 and S5, the energy difference values (δE) between the σ and π orbitals for Mg, N-CQDs and N-CQDs were 1.44 eV and 1.42 eV, respectively. Both of them were lower than 1.50 eV, which was a standard value for the principle of carbene triplet ground state (Gu et al., 2014). Therefore, we could conclude that the fluorescence mechanism for Mg, N-CQDs and N-CQDs samples could follow the principle of carbene triplet ground state. Among of these three states, the surface state of CQDs was an important effector to influence their fluorescence ability, which was usually excited from antibonding orbit of π (π*) to the surface state. Due to the present of Mg and N elements in their structures, Mg, N-CQDs possessed three types of antibonding orbits, including the orbits of carbon core (C π*), Mg (Mg π*) and N (N π*). Meanwhile, the N-CQDs owned C π* and N π* antibonding orbits. The difference in the fluorescence spectra of Mg, N-CQDs and N-CQDs probably was caused by the difference in their antibonding orbits. Therefore, the energies of Mg π* and N π* were obtained through calculating the energy levels of LUMO for these two samples (Tao et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. 3a, the optical band gaps of Mg, N-CQDs and N-CQDs (determined by the absorption edge in their absorption spectra) were approximately 2.43 eV and 2.53 eV, respectively. The oxidation potentials of Mg, N-CQDs and N-CQDs were 0.58 eV and 0.79 eV, respectively (Fig. 6). According to Eq. S2 and S4, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of Mg, N-CQDs were calculated to be -4.63 eV and -2.20 eV, respectively (Chen et al., 2016; Hu et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2014), which were separately calculated to be -4.84 eV and -2.31 eV for N-CQDs. It was found that the LUMO level of Mg, N-CQDs (corresponding to Mg π*) was lower than that of N-CQDs (ascribing to N π*).

The excitons of surface state in the Mg, N-CQDs was usually in the form of transition from Mg π* to surface state, which was transition from N π* to surface state for the N-CQDs. The lower energy levels of the orbit would cause a lower released energy, which eventually extending the emission wavelength (Baker and Baker, 2010; Tao et al., 2017). As illustrated in the above LUMO energy levels, the energy level of Mg π* was lower than that of N π*, which was the main cause for the red shifting in the emission wavelength of Mg, N-CQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , the excitons of surface state in the Mg, N-CQDs were through many ways, e.g., transition from Mg π* to surface state, or from N π* to Mg π* to surface state, which was mainly accounted for the increase of QYs in the Mg, N-CQDs.

In summary, a facile strategy was fabricated for conversion of lignin-based waste into green fluorescence carbon quantum dots via hydrothermal treatment. The as-prepared Mg, N-CQDs possessed plenty of nitrogen and magnesium containing groups, which could exhibit bright green fluorescence with a quantum yield up to 46.38 %. The introduction of Mg element was mainly cause for the green fluorescence emission of Mg, N-CQDs, while the present of Mg and N elements accounted for their high QYs. The Mg, N-CQDs displayed specific chromatic response to the pH values (in the range of 2.2–12.0). The emitted color of Mg, N-CQDs was changed from blue to yellow as the pH values of solutions altered from 12.0–2.2. The change of metal ligand at the edge of Mg, N-CQDs was accounted for their specific chromatic fluorescence response. These findings could provide a potential fluorescence probe from lignin biomass wastes for detecting the pH values of complicated environments.

Xiaoxu Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization. Shiyao Hou: Visualization, Investigation. Tingting Chu: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Project administration. Jiazhi Han: Formal analysis. Runsen Li: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yanzhu Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yumei Gong: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Haiming Li: Writing - review & editing. Zhiwei Wan: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation (22078036 and 31500493), Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC1807232), Liaoning BaiQianWan Talents Program, the Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control (2019KF30) and the State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering (South China University of Technology, No. 202002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.indcrop.2021.113507.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
